title: [lichen_l2.c:437-446] peer_find_locked uses memcmp for timing-safe comparison
description:
What: peer_find_locked() uses memcmp() to compare EUI-64 addresses. memcmp is not constant-time - it returns early on first mismatching byte.

Why it matters: An attacker measuring function timing could potentially infer information about which peer entries exist by observing how quickly lookups fail. However:
1. EUI-64 addresses are 8 bytes, limiting timing difference to ~8 memory accesses
2. EUI-64 is not secret material (it's broadcast in IPv6 headers)
3. The real security boundary is signature verification, not address lookup

This is low priority because EUI-64 is public information.

Evidence:
```c
if (peer_table[i].active &&
    memcmp(peer_table[i].eui64, eui64, LICHEN_EUI64_LEN) == 0) {
    return &peer_table[i];
}
```
close_reason: Documented in peer_find_locked docstring: EUI-64 is public (not secret), so memcmp is fine. Constant-time iteration is already done in peer_table_try_all_peers_locked() for signature verification where timing could leak peer identity.
